title: NOC 65201 – Food counter attendants, kitchen helpers and related support occupations | GTR Immigration
description: NOC 65201 represents the essential workforce powering Canada’s massive fast-food, cafeteria, and restaurant back-of-house operations. As a TEER 5 occupation, it is one of the most accessible entry points for international workers looking to start their Canadian journey. From preparing basic food items to ensuring kitchen hygiene and serving customers at high-volume counters, these professionals keep the Canadian food service industry moving. For immigration, this role is a "high-demand" category for several Provincial Nominee Programs (PNPs) and the Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP).

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category6 – Sales and service occupationsTEER Level5Major Group65 – Sales and service support occupationsSub-Major Group652 – Support occupations in food, accommodation and tourismMinor Group6520 – Food support occupationsNOC Code65201

duties: Take customers' orders and process payments at counters or drive-through windows.
Prepare, heat, and finish simple food items such as sandwiches, salads, and pizzas.
Portion and wrap food and package take-out orders.
Wash, peel, and cut vegetables and fruit to assist cooks.
Clean and sanitize kitchen work areas, equipment, and utensils.
Remove trash and clean kitchen floors and dining areas.
Unpack and store supplies in refrigerators, cupboards, and other storage areas.
employment_requirements: No formal education is usually required, though some secondary school is often preferred.
On-the-job training is provided for all safety and food handling protocols.
Food Safety Certification: Many provinces require a basic food handler's certificate (e.g., FoodSafe in BC).
Ability to work in a fast-paced, physical environment is essential.
Basic proficiency in English or French is required for safety and customer interaction.
job_outlook: The outlook for this occupation is Favourable. Projections indicate over 150,000 new job openings nationally due to industry expansion and the natural progression of current workers into higher TEER levels. The sector is a cornerstone of the Canadian youth and newcomer employment market.
labour_market_demand: The demand for food counter attendants and kitchen helpers is Very Good across almost every province. In 2026, labor shortages in the hospitality sector remain a challenge for Canadian employers, particularly in Ontario, British Columbia, and Alberta. This has led to an increase in LMIA (Labour Market Impact Assessment) approvals for this NOC, making it a reliable route for those seeking employer-sponsored work permits.
Rapid Employment: High turnover and constant expansion mean you can often secure a job offer quickly.
Skill Foundations: Gain foundational experience in Canadian food safety and customer service standards.
Path to Promotion: Transition into Cook (NOC 63200) or Food Service Supervisor (NOC 62020) roles after gaining experience.
PR Accessibility: Many provinces have "Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled" streams specifically designed to help workers in this NOC transition to Permanent Residency.
pathways: BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled (ELSS): A dedicated pathway for those working in the food and beverage sector for 9 consecutive months in BC.
Alberta Tourism and Hospitality Stream: A priority stream for workers in eligible hospitality occupations.
Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): A direct route for workers with job offers in the Atlantic provinces (NS, NB, PEI, NL).
Manitoba Skilled Worker Stream: For those who have worked for at least 6 months with an employer in the province.
